catalog_note: Seed controls for harness development. These are local operational controls aligned to upstream frameworks; they are not authoritative AISVS requirement IDs.

controls.title: Inventory AI system boundaries and trust decisions
controls.summary: The system has a documented architecture, trust boundaries, data flows, model/provider dependencies, and explicit AI-mediated decisions.

alignments.note: Architecture and lifecycle verification
Governance, inventory, and risk management
Govern and Map functions

evidence.prompt: Attach a current architecture diagram showing users, services, models, tools, data stores, and external providers.
Describe prompt, context, retrieval, model output, tool-call, and logging data flows.
List decisions or recommendations made or mediated by the AI system.

tests.prompt: Review whether every trust boundary has an owner, input validation strategy, and monitoring expectation.

hardening_actions: Create a living AI system inventory entry.
Document where user-controlled content can influence prompts, context, tools, and outputs.
Identify which decisions require human approval.

controls.title: Constrain prompt and instruction boundaries
controls.summary: System, developer, user, retrieved, and tool-supplied content are separated and protected against cross-boundary instruction injection.

alignments.note: Prompt and input/output handling verification
Prompt injection and sensitive information disclosure
Adversarial prompt and input manipulation scenarios

evidence.prompt: Attach the system/developer prompt contract or instruction hierarchy.
Attach prompt injection, indirect prompt injection, and context confusion test results.
Describe how retrieved or user-controlled content is labeled and isolated from trusted instructions.

tests.prompt: Inject malicious instructions into a retrieval source and verify they cannot override trusted instructions.
Attempt to make user content appear as system/developer instructions.

hardening_actions: Label untrusted content in prompts and tool responses.
Use structured prompt templates with explicit instruction hierarchy.
Regression-test known jailbreak and indirect injection patterns.

controls.title: Authorize and audit agent tool use
controls.summary: Tools available to AI agents are inventoried, scoped, authorized, logged, and constrained by least privilege.

alignments.note: Agentic action security and authorization
Identity, access, logging, and change control
Excessive agency and supply chain risks

evidence.prompt: List all tools, plugins, MCP servers, APIs, and external actions available to the agent.
Describe scopes, approval requirements, policy checks, and deny conditions for each tool.
Attach sample logs that show tool request, approval, execution, output, and actor context.

tests.prompt: Attempt to trigger a tool outside the user's role, tenant, environment, or approval boundary.
Attempt replay or tampering of a prior tool invocation.

hardening_actions: Use an allowlist for agent tools and permissions.
Require human approval for destructive, financial, privileged, or external side-effect actions.
Log tool calls with correlation IDs and user/session context.

controls.title: Protect sensitive data in prompts, retrieval, and outputs
controls.summary: Sensitive data exposure is minimized across prompt construction, context retrieval, model responses, logs, and evaluation datasets.

alignments.note: Data protection and privacy verification
Sensitive information disclosure
Data security, privacy, and governance

evidence.prompt: List data classifications processed by prompts, embeddings, retrieval sources, model providers, and logs.
Describe filtering, redaction, tokenization, masking, or policy controls before data reaches the model.
Document retention and deletion behavior for prompts, completions, embeddings, and logs.

tests.prompt: Seed canary secrets and verify they are not retrieved, emitted, logged, or sent to unauthorized providers.
Verify tenant or role boundaries cannot be bypassed through semantic retrieval.

hardening_actions: Block secrets and high-risk regulated data from prompts unless explicitly approved.
Apply retrieval filters before context assembly.
Review provider retention and training-use settings.

controls.title: Govern retrieval sources and knowledge freshness
controls.summary: RAG and context sources are authorized, access-controlled, freshness-scored, and resistant to poisoning.

alignments.note: Knowledge base and data lifecycle verification
RAG poisoning and supply chain considerations
Data poisoning and input manipulation scenarios

evidence.prompt: List retrieval sources, owners, indexes, update cadence, access model, and freshness expectations.
Describe ingestion validation, provenance, malware scanning, and poisoning detection.
Attach tests proving retrieval respects user, tenant, and document-level authorization.

tests.prompt: Insert hostile instructions into an authorized document and verify context handling prevents instruction takeover.
Verify stale or revoked content is excluded or labeled in generated answers.

hardening_actions: Track source provenance and freshness metadata.
Apply authorization before retrieval and before prompt assembly.
Quarantine newly ingested high-risk content until validated.

controls.title: Validate output before privileged or external action
controls.summary: Model output is treated as untrusted until validated, especially before code execution, ticket changes, emails, transactions, deployments, or cloud actions.

alignments.note: Output handling and agent action verification
Excessive agency and insecure output handling
Change control, logging, and human oversight

evidence.prompt: Document validators, schemas, policy checks, and approval gates used before action.
List actions that require human approval and the identity/role required to approve.
Attach tests for command injection, unsafe code, unauthorized data changes, and malformed tool arguments.

tests.prompt: Attempt to cause malformed or policy-violating tool arguments and verify the call is blocked.
Verify destructive actions require explicit human approval and are fully logged.

hardening_actions: Use schemas and policy checks for all tool arguments.
Treat generated code and commands as untrusted input.
Require approval for destructive or externally visible actions.

controls.title: Monitor abuse, drift, and security-relevant behavior
controls.summary: The system emits security telemetry for prompts, retrieval, tool use, denials, policy decisions, model/provider failures, and anomalous behavior.

alignments.note: Monitoring, logging, and operations verification
Logging, monitoring, incident response, and assurance
Measure and Manage functions

evidence.prompt: Document security events, metrics, traces, and alerts emitted by the AI system.
Attach prompt abuse, tool abuse, leakage, and anomalous usage detections.
Attach AI-specific incident response, rollback, kill-switch, or provider failover procedures.

tests.prompt: Verify blocked prompt/tool events are logged without leaking sensitive content.
Exercise feature disablement or provider isolation for an AI safety/security incident.

hardening_actions: Add correlation IDs across prompt, retrieval, model, and tool-call events.
Create detections for repeated prompt injection, blocked tools, leakage attempts, and policy bypasses.
Define rollback and disablement paths before production launch.

controls.title: Manage AI supply chain and provider risk
controls.summary: Models, providers, SDKs, plugins, prompts, evaluation datasets, and agent dependencies are tracked and reviewed for supply chain risk.

alignments.note: Supply chain and third-party component verification
Supply chain vulnerabilities
Third-party risk, change management, and assurance

evidence.prompt: List model providers, model IDs, regions, retention settings, and contractual/security assumptions.
Attach SDK, plugin, MCP server, prompt package, and agent dependency inventory.
Attach provider security review, DPA, terms review, or vendor risk assessment.

tests.prompt: Verify production model/provider configuration matches approved retention, region, and data-use settings.
Verify plugins, MCP servers, and agent tools are pinned, reviewed, and monitored for change.

hardening_actions: Pin model and SDK versions where feasible.
Track prompt templates and tool definitions as versioned artifacts.
Review provider data-use, retention, logging, and incident notification terms.

controls.title: Perform STRIDE threat modeling with AI-specific abuse cases
controls.summary: The system has a documented STRIDE threat model that captures business context, current and target state, trust boundaries, AI-specific abuse cases, control gaps, compensating controls, residual risk, and remediation decisions.

alignments.note: Threat modeling, architecture, and security requirement verification
Risk mapping for LLM and GenAI applications
Risk management, governance, and assurance evidence
Adversary scenario and abuse-case development
What are we working on, what can go wrong, what are we going to do about it, and did we do a good job

evidence.prompt: Attach the AI threat model with scope, owners, business context, assets, trust boundaries, current/target state, and review tier.
Attach STRIDE analysis covering spoofing, tampering, repudiation, information disclosure, denial of service, and elevation of privilege.
Attach or link system context, trust-zone/environment, IAM/access flow, data flow, and AI tool/retrieval flow views.
Attach mitigation decisions, owners, due dates, compensating controls, accepted risks, and residual risk.
Document whether the review answered the four-question frame and included the right engineering, product, security, data, and operations stakeholders.

tests.prompt: Verify every STRIDE category was considered and irrelevant categories have rationale rather than being silently skipped.
Verify prompt injection, indirect prompt injection, retrieval poisoning, data leakage, unauthorized tool use, excessive agency, and unsafe output handling were considered where applicable.
Verify each high or medium risk maps to a remediation, compensating control, accepted risk, or explicit release-blocking decision.

hardening_actions: Run the baseline assisted STRIDE flow during design review.
Use the advanced Shostack-style four-question frame for high-impact, sensitive, production-facing, or agentic systems.
Track unresolved risks in the implementation ticket, risk register, or exception workflow.
Define revalidation triggers for design changes, new tools, new retrieval sources, new data classes, incidents, and exception renewals.

controls.title: Issue security advisory before review meeting
controls.summary: Early project requests receive a structured advisory that asks the owning team to document use case, data inventory, data flows, trust boundaries, control assumptions, and open risks before Security performs a risk review.

alignments.note: Security requirements, architecture evidence, and review readiness
Threat modeling intake and structured review preparation
Governance, data handling, and assurance evidence
Govern, Map, and Manage functions

evidence.prompt: Attach the advisory response sent to the requestor, including the requested self-service threat model or review packet.
Attach the requestor-owned review packet with use case, scope, data inventory, data flow, trust boundaries, controls, and open risks.
Document data classifications, regulated data scope, sensitive metadata, retention, logging, export paths, and non-production restrictions.
Document whether a meeting is needed and which unresolved risks, unclear data flows, sensitive-data concerns, exception needs, or launch-blocking decisions remain.

tests.prompt: Verify the review packet covers use case, data inventory, data flow, trust boundaries, controls, known assumptions, and open risks.
Verify every collected, stored, logged, exported, or derived data category has a classification and owner.
Verify any scheduled security meeting is tied to unresolved risks, unclear data flows, sensitive-data handling concerns, exception needs, or launch-blocking decisions.

hardening_actions: Send a concise advisory response before scheduling discovery-heavy meetings.
Ask engineering to publish the self-service threat model or review packet first.
Focus Security review on actual risks, gaps, assumptions, and decisions.
Reserve meetings for unresolved risks, unclear data flows, sensitive-data handling concerns, exception needs, or launch-blocking decisions.
